title: Topologically Enforced Lifshitz Multicriticality in One Dimension
abstract: Recent advances have revealed that topology can further enrich the universality classes of quantum phase transitions, thereby extending beyond the traditional paradigms of statistical and condensed matter physics. However, multicriticality between topologically distinct quantum critical lines remains insufficiently explored. In this Letter, we systematically construct and investigate a novel class of topologically enforced Lifshitz multicritical points in one dimensional chiral symmetric fermionic systems. Such multicriticality is driven solely by changes in the topology of neighboring critical lines, beyond previously recognized multicritical points that are typically induced by changes in critical exponents. More importantly, the topologically enforced multicriticality identified here can host robust topological degeneracies while surprisingly exhibiting a breakdown of the Li Haldane bulk boundary correspondence-a phenomenon we elucidate through a simple physical picture.
summary: The paper introduces a novel class of Lifshitz Multicritical Points driven purely by changes in the topology of neighboring quantum critical lines. Using entanglement spectroscopy and energy spectrum analysis, the authors prove that these topologically enforced points exhibit a breakdown of the Li–Haldane correspondence. This mismatch between bulk topological signatures and boundary modes offers a new theoretical handle for characterizing exotic quantum criticality.
main_problem: To investigate novel Lifshitz Multicritical Points (MCPs) whose existence is dictated solely by changes in the topology of adjacent quantum critical lines, rather than traditional changes in critical exponents.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that at these topologically enforced MCPs, a breakdown of the Li–Haldane bulk-boundary correspondence occurs, evidenced by a mismatch between topological signatures found in the entanglement spectrum (PBC) and the absence of protected zero modes in the physical energy spectrum (OBC).
method: The analysis employs advanced theoretical diagnostics including calculating the bulk entanglement spectrum using correlation matrices and comparing these results against single-particle OBC energy spectra derived from low-energy effective Hamiltonians.
model_or_system: The study focuses on one-dimensional chiral-symmetric fermionic systems, modeled by generalized hopping structures ($\alpha$ chain models) that allow for tuning between different critical lines to induce the Lifshitz MCPs.
key_observables: Topological degeneracy (midgap entanglement levels), central charges ($c$), dynamical exponent ($z$), and the comparison count of midgap pairs vs. OBC zero modes.
important_parameters: The hopping parameters ($\alpha, \alpha'$), the coupling range width ($R$), and the relative shift parameter ($s$) are crucial for determining the topological nature of the MCP.
main_assumptions: The analysis is primarily conducted in the Renormalization Group (RG) limit, treating the system via low-energy effective theories derived from free-fermion models.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate phase diagrams showing the transition to topologically enforced MCPs; comparisons show PBC spectra with midgap levels but OBC spectra lacking corresponding zero modes at these novel points.
paper_structure: The paper systematically constructs the Hamiltonian, analyzes the topological invariants (winding numbers) associated with adjacent critical lines, and then uses entanglement/energy spectrum diagnostics to prove the breakdown of the Li–Haldane correspondence specifically at the topologically enforced MCPs.
why_interesting: This work provides a powerful new diagnostic tool—the comparison between PBC entanglement spectra and OBC edge modes—to probe topological phases in systems where standard invariants fail, which is highly relevant for understanding boundary physics in condensed matter theory.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Phase diagram of transitions between quantum crit- ical lines. (a) Representative phase diagram for a transition between two topologically distinct critical lines, illustrated by the minimal case α = 0 and α′ = 1. The endpoint in- sets show the corresponding auxiliary-root configurations: a root on the unit circle signals criticality, while an additional root inside the unit circle encodes nontrivial topological de- generacy. The intermediate MCP exhibits zdyn = 2 scaling. (b) Auxiliary-root representation of the phase transition. In moving between the two critical lines, the roots are transferred from inside to outside the unit circle. When these roots meet the original critical...

FIG. 2. Bulk entanglement spectra and boundary energy spectrum near a Lifshitz MCP separating distinct univer- sality classes. PBC entanglement spectra at two adjacent topologically trivial quantum critical points: the Ising criti- cal point (a) and the XY critical point (b). The insets show the corresponding entanglement entropy scaling, giving cen- tral charges c ≃0.50 and c ≃1.00, respectively. (c) PBC entanglement spectrum at the conventional Lifshitz MCP. No isolated midgap entanglement level appears. (d) OBC single- particle energy spectrum at the same MCP. The inset zooms into an exponentially small zero-energy window, showing the absence of boundary zero modes.

FIG. 3. Bulk entanglement spectra and physical boundary spectrum near a topologically enforced Lifshitz MCP. PBC entanglement spectra at two adjacent quantum critical points: a topologically trivial critical point (a) and a topologically nontrivial critical point (b). The insets show the correspond- ing entanglement entropy scaling, both giving the same cen- tral charge c ≃1. (c) PBC entanglement spectrum at the topologically enforced Lifshitz MCP, where midgap entangle- ment levels appear. (d) OBC energy spectrum at the same MCP. The inset zooms into a small window around zero en- ergy and shows no zero-energy modes. Thus, although the PBC entanglement spectrum develops midgap levels,...

FIG. 4. RG-limit picture for the breakdown of the Li–Haldane correspondence. Each oval denotes one physical unit cell, con- taining an Aj site on the upper row and a Bj site on the lower row. The map T denotes a relative translation of the B sublattice, Bj →Bj−1, which multiplies the off-diagonal Bloch element vk by eik under PBC and thereby increases the winding number by one. The colored bonds indicate different hopping amplitudes in the real-space coupling pattern, with the green bonds having twice the amplitude of the blue bonds.
